g.data-narration: Welcome to Module 4 on Accessing I/O Devices. This module covers how the CPU communicates with peripherals.
I/O devices allow computers to communicate with the external world. Understanding I/O access methods impacts system performance.
g.text: Input/Output devices are peripheral components that allow a computer to communicate with the
external world. The methods of I/O access significantly impact system performance and complexity.

g.data-narration: I/O devices are classified into four main types: Input, Output, Storage, and Communication devices.
I/O access differs from memory access in speed, data transfer complexity, and error handling requirements.
An I/O interface connects devices to the system bus using Data, Status, Control registers and an Address Decoder.
Programmed I/O uses polling where the CPU continuously checks device status until ready for data transfer.
g.text: The CPU continuously polls (checks) the status of an I/O device to see if it's ready
for data transfer. CPU busy-waits until the device is ready.

g.data-narration: Here's pseudocode showing how polling works: CPU checks status bit in a loop until device is ready.
Interrupt-Driven I/O: Device notifies CPU when ready via interrupt signal, allowing CPU to do other tasks.
g.text: Instead of polling, the device sends an interrupt signal to the CPU when ready.
The CPU can perform other tasks until interrupted.

g.data-narration: Interrupt processing involves: Request, Acknowledge, Save Context, Execute ISR, Restore Context, Resume.
Direct Memory Access (DMA) allows devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU intervention.
g.text: DMA allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without continuous
CPU intervention. Very efficient for large data transfers.

g.data-narration: DMA has three transfer modes: Burst (fastest, blocks CPU), Cycle Stealing (moderate), and Transparent (slowest, no CPU impact).
g.text: Transfers entire block at once | CPU blocked | Fastest
Transfers one word at a time | Steals bus cycles | Moderate
Transfers when CPU idle | No cycle stealing | Slowest

g.data-narration: Memory-Mapped I/O assigns device registers to memory addresses, using regular memory instructions for I/O access.
g.text: I/O devices share the same address space as memory. Device registers are assigned
memory addresses. Accessed using normal memory instructions (LOAD, STORE).

g.data-narration: I/O-Mapped (Isolated) I/O uses separate address space with special IN and OUT instructions for device access.
g.text: Separate address space for I/O devices. Requires special I/O instructions (IN, OUT).
Full memory address space available. Clear separation between I/O and memory.

g.data-narration: Synchronous I/O: CPU waits for completion. Asynchronous I/O: CPU continues, signaled via interrupt when done.
g.text: CPU waits for completion • Simple • Example: Programmed I/O
CPU continues execution • Better performance • Example: Interrupt, DMA

g.data-narration: Handshaking coordinates data transfer between devices using Data Valid and Data Accepted signals.
g.text: Handshaking coordinates data transfer between devices of different speeds using control signals.

g.data-narration: Bus Arbitration determines which device gets bus control when multiple devices need access simultaneously.
g.text: When multiple devices need bus access, arbitration determines which device gets control.

g.data-narration: Summary: We covered three I/O access methods - Programmed, Interrupt-Driven, and DMA. Also learned about Memory-Mapped and I/O-Mapped addressing.
Understanding I/O access methods is essential for system performance. Choose the right method based on your application's speed and complexity needs.
g.text: Efficient I/O operations are critical to overall system performance. The choice of I/O access
method depends on speed requirements, hardware complexity, and CPU utilization needs.
